stem: The definite integral \( \int_{a}^{b} h(x)\, dx \) is defined as the limit of Riemann sums \( \lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h(x_i)\,\Delta x \). Evaluate \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left( ([[r]])x^{2} + ([[p]])x + ([[q]]) \right) dx \).
solution: Antidifferentiating term by term gives \( F(x) = \frac{[[r]]}{3}x^{3} + \frac{[[p]]}{2}x^{2} + ([[q]])x \); the Riemann-sum limit equals \( F([[b]]) - F([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the antiderivative only at the upper limit, omitting the lower-limit term.
This applies the power rule but forgets to divide each term by its new exponent.
This is \( h([[b]]) - h([[a]]) \), the change in the integrand, not its integral.
This is off by the width of the interval.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: By definition, \( \int_{0}^{[[hi]]} h(x)\, dx = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h(x_i)\,\Delta x \) for any partition of \( [0, [[hi]]] \) into \( n \) pieces. Use this to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[hi]]} \left( ([[A]])\cos(x) + ([[B]]) \right) dx \).
solution: An antiderivative of the integrand is \( F(x) = ([[A]])\sin(x) + ([[B]])x \), so the Riemann-sum limit equals \( F([[hi]]) - F(0) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \sin([[hi]]) \) directly as the antiderivative value without subtracting the value at 0, and misses that \( \sin \) at a multiple of \( \pi \) is 0.
This mistakenly antidifferentiates \( \cos(x) \) as \( x \) instead of \( \sin(x) \).
This drops the contribution from the cosine term entirely.
This is off by the amplitude \( [[A]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By definition, \( \int_{0}^{b} h(x)\, dx \) is the limit, as \( n\to\infty \), of Riemann sums \( \sum_{i=1}^{n} h(x_i)\,\Delta x \) over \( [0,b] \). Using this definition (i.e. the resulting Fundamental Theorem computation), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[hi]]} ([[A]])\,2^{[[k]]x}\, dx \), given that \( 2^{[[k]]\cdot [[hi]]} = [[m]] \).
solution: An antiderivative is \( F(x) = \dfrac{[[A]]\cdot 2^{[[k]]x}}{[[k]]\ln 2} \), so the limit equals \( F([[hi]]) - F(0) = \dfrac{[[A]]([[m]]-1)}{[[k]]\ln 2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( [[k]]\ln 2 \) when antidifferentiating the exponential.
This uses \( [[m]] \) instead of \( [[m]]-1 \) in the numerator, forgetting to subtract the value at \( x=0 \).
This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it.
This treats the integrand as constant and multiplies by the width \( [[hi]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
